retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Transposons -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial transposons belong to two classes: compound transposons (class I) and complex transposons (class II). A class I transposon contains a copy of an insertion sequence (IS) at each end. At its ends, a class II transposon has two equal sequences of 30/40 pairs of nucleotides each, arranged in the opposite orientation.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Transposons -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers have found transposons in the genomes of eukaryotes and prokaryotes. Genomes of eukaryotes contain multitudes of repetitive DNA sequences. Transposons make up much of this repetitive DNA. 45% of the human genome is transposable elements. [4] Because so much of the genome is transposable, the theory is that they are participants in speciation and evolution, although the triggers are not understood. [5] Transposons have a similar structure to non-transposable DNA and contain the same nucleic acids and nucleotides (cytosine, adenine, guanine, and thymine).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The term "nonfunctional pseudogenes" is also a concern when defining the relationship between pseudogenes and their parental copies. One study found that nitric oxide synthase (NOS) in the snail family, Lymnaea stagnalis , and its pseudogene have an inhibitory relationship with each other. The NOS pseudogene transcript was revealed to act as an antisense RNA through hybridization and decrease the expression of the functional mRNA. However, the NOS pseudogene itself has numerous defects and is unable to code for an actual protein like its functional copy. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, due to the diverse and vast amount of pseudogenes sharing similar coding sequences to their functional gene counterparts, there is no standard method for identifying them without identifying the entire genome. However, many independent groups are currently working to resolve this dilemma and create a standardized and efficient means of pseudogene identification.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Transposons -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Transposons can be divided based on the mechanism of transposition and integration. Class I elements, retrotransposons, use an RNA intermediate that is reverse-transcribed into a cDNA copy that reintegrates into a new genomic home.  Class II elements are DNA transposons mobilized by a DNA intermediate or by replication involving a circular DNA intermediate. Each of these classes can further sub-categorize based on common genetic or monophyletic origins.

retrieved_docs.title: Butterfly, Moth, and Caterpillar Envenomation -- Pathophysiology -- Classification of Ophthalmia Nodosa (Cadera et al)
retrieved_docs.content: Seasonal ataxia is a clinical syndrome of acute cerebellar ataxia, nausea, and vomiting. This condition has been implicated in epidemics in western Nigeria during the 1950s, caused by consuming the African silkworm ( Anapha venata ) for protein. [49] [18] [50] [51] Coinciding with the rainy season (July-October), the larvae are abundantly found in local markets and frequently consumed with high carbohydrate-based foods, which contain thiamine-binding cyanogenetic glycosides. [51] However, the larvae of the Anapha venata contain heat-resistant thiaminases that exacerbate the thiamine-deficient population. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing -- Random and Extensive Mutagenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Primers randomly produced with mismatched bases. This technique synthesizes a set of mutagenic primers with 3 mismatched bases at a single base position in the same reaction. These sets of primers are then used to synthesize mutant DNA with 3 different mutations at the same base position. Other than this, a primer can be synthesized using an ambiguous base (eg, deoxyinosine), capable of base-pairing with varying dNTPs. In theory, there is a 75% probability a wrong base pairs with this ambiguous base, which generates mutations in subsequent rounds of replication.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Testing -- Random and Extensive Mutagenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Use of ambiguous base analogs (eg, deoxyinosine). As already stated, deoxyinosine triphosphate (dI) can base pair with different dNTPs. In a reaction that contains dI and adjusted dNTPs concentrations (eg, typical levels of 3 dNTPs and low levels of the fourth dNTP), a dI is likely to be incorporated into the newly synthesized sequence. Theoretically, there is a 75% chance that a wrong base pairs with a dI during subsequent replication events. This technique can result in 1 in 250 base pair mutation rates. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Genetics Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: In manual Sanger sequencing, the user reads all 4 gel lanes simultaneously, moving from bottom to top to identify the terminal ddNTP for each band. For instance, if the bottom band is found in the ddGTP column, then the smallest PCR fragment terminates with ddGTP, and the first nucleotide from the 5’ end of the original sequence has a guanine (G) base. [42] Automated Sanger sequencing employs a computer to read each capillary gel band sequentially, using fluorescence to determine the terminal ddNTP identity. Laser activation of fluorescent tags emits light, detected by the computer, with each ddNTP tagged with a unique fluorescent label. The output is a chromatogram displaying fluorescent peaks corresponding to each nucleotide along the template DNA's length. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) analogues are classified as members of the exenatid family, indicating that they share the structural and pharmacologic characteristics of exenatid compounds, which function as GLP‑1 receptor agonists commonly used in the treatment of type 2 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon is a peptide hormone that is composed of amino acids, placing it within the broader category of amino acids, peptides, or proteins; therefore, glucagon is classified as a member of this protein‑based group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are organic, nitrogen‑containing carboxylic acids that serve as fundamental protein building blocks and as precursors for several neurotransmitters and neuroregulators, including dopamine and acetylcholine. Representative members of the amino‑acid family include proline (a prototypical parent), glycine, levodopa (L‑DOPA), chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids, and S‑adenosyl‑L‑homocysteine, each highlighting specialized biochemical roles. Because of their precursor function, amino acids are explicitly linked to the “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” subcluster and to neurotransmitter synthesis pathways in general. Acetylcholine exemplifies the overlap between chemical classification and function: it is a nitrogen‑containing compound, a neurotransmitter and neuroregulator, and the active moiety of the ophthalmic preparation acetylcholine chloride 10 mg/mL (Miochol). Adenosine is also classified as a neurotransmitter and is derived from S‑adenosyl‑L‑homocysteine. Dopaminergic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine and deltorphin I, Ala(2)‑ are both peptide‑derived entities that are mapped from the oligopeptidy class, indicating that each compound originates from or is structurally related to oligopeptides. This relationship highlights their common biochemical foundation in short peptide sequences, which may be relevant for their pharmacological properties or research applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, encompassing cyanocobalamin and its analogues, includes several distinct cobalamin forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, which are recognized as member compounds within this vitamin family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: action is mediated by electrolyte modulation. Its broader relevance is highlighted by its fundamental presence in natural structures such as perleť, illustrating the compound’s versatility across pharmacological and biological domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is classified as a neurotransmitter, reflecting its fundamental role in chemical signaling within the nervous system. Acetylcholine chloride, a salt form of acetylcholine, represents a more specific, narrower entity derived from acetylcholine and therefore falls under the broader category of neurotransmitters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroregulators subcluster encompasses both amino acids and acetylcholine, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of these molecules in neuropharmacology and psychology. Additionally, the supplement N.O.-Xplode is mapped to the amino‑acid category, suggesting it contains or is related to amino‑acid components. Collectively, this network illustrates how amino‑acid derivatives serve as precursors or direct agents for major neurotransmitter systems, including dopaminergic and cholinergic pathways, and how various pharmacological agents (e.g., dopaminergic drugs, acetylcholine salts) are organized within these interconnected hierarchies, informing both biochemical understanding and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonukleasy, an enzyme that catalyzes the cleavage of DNA strands, encompasses within its broader class the more specific enzyme streptodornase; streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of deoxyribonukleasy with distinct functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 283
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine, deltorphin I (Ala2‑) and telaprevir are all compounds that are mapped from or derived from oligopeptidy, indicating a shared origin in peptide‑based structures. This common derivation suggests that these agents may possess related biochemical characteristics typical of oligopeptide‑derived molecules, which can be relevant for understanding their pharmacologic behavior and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1620
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is a naturally occurring alkaloid, indicating that it belongs to the class of alkaloids that are found in nature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Tropatepine possesses a muscarinic receptor antagonist disposition, indicating that it functions as an antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Benoxinate is chemically related to prokain, with benoxinate being mapped from prokain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2736
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny, indicating that it belongs to the gonadotropin family of hormones.
